coredata.dc:description: Developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the solvatochromic effect and exploring multifunctional applications remains challenging. Herein, robust solvatochromic carbon quantum dots (RS-CQDs) with emission shift up to ∼62 nm from yellow to red was fabricated by the hydrothermal method. The RS-CQDs was used to detect water and Sn4+ in the linear ranges and limits of detection of 2.0–97.6% and 0.14% and 6.24–53.18 μM and 66.3 nM, respectively, and was further applied to determine Sn4+ in practical water samples with satisfactory results. In addition, RS-CQDs exhibited bright red emission in oil media with a 9.7-fold increase in fluorescence relative to aqueous media, making them a wash-free probe for specifically staining lipids. Compared to the commercial lipid marker BODIPY 493/503, the RS-CQDs-based probe has significant advantages, such as longer emission, larger Stokes shift, and better photostability, ensuring that RS-CQDs-based marker can implement real-time and wash-free monitoring and imaging of lipids in living cells, liver tissues, zebrafish embryos, and zebrafish larvae. This study provides a novel research direction for the development of metal-doped CQDs by demonstrating RS-CQDs as the viability of fluorescence probes for water and Sn4+ detection and the efficiency of RS-CQDs as a fluorescent marker for lipid imaging.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the solvatochromic effect and exploring multifunctional applications remains challenging. Herein, robust solvatochromic carbon quantum dots (RS-CQDs) with emission shift up to ∼62 nm from yellow to red was fabricated by the hydrothermal method. The RS-CQDs was used to detect water and Sn4+ in the linear ranges and limits of detection of 2.0–97.6% and 0.14% and 6.24–53.18 μM and 66.3 nM, respectively, and was further applied to determine Sn4+ in practical water samples with satisfactory results. In addition, RS-CQDs exhibited bright red emission in oil media with a 9.7-fold increase in fluorescence relative to aqueous media, making them a wash-free probe for specifically staining lipids. Compared to the commercial lipid marker BODIPY 493/503, the RS-CQDs-based probe has significant advantages, such as longer emission, larger Stokes shift, and better photostability, ensuring that RS-CQDs-based marker can implement real-time and wash-free monitoring and imaging of lipids in living cells, liver tissues, zebrafish embryos, and zebrafish larvae. This study provides a novel research direction for the development of metal-doped CQDs by demonstrating RS-CQDs as the viability of fluorescence probes for water and Sn4+ detection and the efficiency of RS-CQDs as a fluorescent marker for lipid imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel photoluminescent material with exceptional optical, electrical, and chemical properties, have a wide range of potential applications (Chen et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020a; Miao et al., 2020). To improve the chemical composition and structural properties of CQDs, heteroatomic doping is regarded as a robust and universal method (Feng et al., 2021), especially metal atom-doped CQDs have become an area of intense research (Li and Dong, 2018; Li et al., 2022a). Metal atoms such as zinc (Zn) (Wu et al., 2018), gadolinium (Gd) (Wang et al., 2022), magnesium (Mg) (Li et al., 2021), copper (Cu) (Liu et al., 2020b), iron (Fe) (Wang et al., 2020b), germanium (Ge) (Liu et al., 2018), hafnium (Hf) (Su et al., 2020a), europium (Eu) (Wang and Chen, 2018), zirconium (Zr) (Su et al., 2020b), gallium (Ga) (Yuan et al., 2020), and cobalt (Co) (Yang et al., 2019) have been used as dopants to synthesize metal atom-doped CQDs, which have shown superior performance in sensing, imaging, phototherapy, optoelectronics, catalysis, etc (Li et al., 2022b). Among the many remarkable properties of metal-doped CQDs, the solvent effect of the metal-doped CQDs has never before been reported. Few nonmetallic-doped CQDs have a solvent effect and are utilized for detecting water (Wu et al., 2019) or metal ions (Zhu et al., 2018), lipid droplet imaging (Gao et al., 2021), etc. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to fabricate metal-doped CQDs with solvent effect, which can diversify the types of solvatochromic CQDs and expand their range of applications.

Water, an essential component of human life, is essential to human survival and industrial production (Ni et al., 2022). However, water is generally considered the most prevalent impurity in organic solvents (Xiao et al., 2021). Especially for certain organic chemical reactions, the trace amount of water in the solvent significantly affects the reaction process, which may directly influence the reaction's efficacy and product formation (Wang et al., 2020a). To eliminate the influence of water in chemical and industrial processes, the sensitive and rapid detection of water content in organic environments is of utmost importance. Compared to many traditional methods for water detection (Karl Fischer titration (Cheng et al., 2019), photometry (Chen et al., 2017), and electrochemistry method (Ohira et al., 2012)), the fluorescence method is regarded as a practical, dependable, and promising detection strategy, owing to its simple preparation, high sensitivity, rapid analysis speed, and straightforward real-time detection (Huang et al., 2016). The blue fluorescence carbon dots were used to detect water content between 0.0% and 55.0% (Ni et al., 2022). Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2020a) developed a dual-emission system for detecting water with a linear range of 0.0–5.0% based on r-CDs (red fluorescence carbon dots) and b-CDs (blue fluorescence carbon dots). Although some CQDs have been used for water detection, these CQDs have some flaws, including a short emission wavelength, low sensitivity, and a narrow linear range. Therefore, we intend to develop solvatochromic CQDs with red fluorescence to detect water, thereby enhancing the detection sensitivity, extending the linear range, and expanding the application of CQDs in water detection.

Stannum (Sn) is utilized extensively in chemical reagents, agriculture, chemical weapons, and industrial processes (Yang et al., 2017). The detection of Sn2+ has been reported (Gao et al., 2020b; Ravichandiran et al., 2021a, 2021c), while fewer studies were used to detect Sn4+. Sn4+ deficiency is already thought to be an important risk factor for bereft hearing, reduced muscle growth, and limited cancer prevention (Ravichandiran et al., 2021d). Similarly, the excessive accumulation of Sn4+ in humans or animals can cause severe neurotoxicity, immunotoxicity, and gastrointestinal diseases (Liu et al., 2020a). However, due to the lack of effective tools for identifying and sensing Sn4+ in biological samples, the toxicological mechanism of Sn4+ in organisms has not been fully elucidated. Therefore, it is significant to spare no effort to explore the recognition and sensing of Sn4+. Acridine-diphenylacetyl moieties with good water solubility and compatibility were used to determine Sn4+ in water and biological media with fast response time (less than 10 s) and high sensitivity (0.268 μM) (Ravichandiran et al., 2021d). Boron and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots have also been designed to detect Sn4+ in a linear range of 0.2–18 μM (Tabaraki et al., 2017). Although a few probes for detecting Sn4+ have been reported, these probes inevitably suffer from narrow linear ranges, high detection limits, and poor sensitivity. Consequently, we believe it is very meaningful and urgent to construct a fluorescent probe for detecting Sn4+ using simple and readily available CQDs.

Lipids, one of the essential nutrients in the human body, are necessary components of cells and tissues and a source of energy and essential fatty acids (Sun et al., 2021). Many diseases, such as obesity, fatty liver, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes, may be closely associated with abnormal lipid levels in the body (Wang et al., 2020a). Consequently, understanding the level of lipids in the human body and imaging lipids are crucial for the diagnosis of human diseases. Numerous imaging techniques, including ultrasound (Jaeschke et al., 2017), magnetic resonance imaging (Ghiani et al., 2017), positron emission tomography (Wagener et al., 2018), X-ray angiography (Matthäus et al., 2018), and optical imaging (Wijesooriya et al., 2019), have been developed to study lipid imaging. Even though these imaging modalities have their own benefits for understanding lipid levels and disease states, their disadvantages, such as high cost, limited penetration depth, and short imaging time, cannot be overlooked (Arlauckas et al., 2019). Consequently, it is crucial to advance lipid research by developing a novel, rapid, label-free, and non-invasive lipid imaging probe based on the biocompatible CQDs described previously.

Hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine (PPD), manganese chloride (MnCl2), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) produced robust solvatochromic carbon quantum dots (RS-CQDs) co-doped with manganese, chlorine, and nitrogen in this study ( Scheme 1). Based on RS-CQDs, their analysis, detection, and imaging applications have been studied in depth. On the one hand, RS-CQDs have been used as a novel fluorescence probe for detecting water and Sn4+, which has achieved satisfactory results in detecting Sn4+ in various actual samples. In contrast, because RS-CQDs have a significantly different fluorescence quantum yield in water and capryl alcohol (CA, the structure is similar to lipid) (Liu et al., 2021), they can be successfully used in lipid imaging in living cells, mouse liver tissues, zebrafish embryos, and zebrafish larvae, which is of great importance to lipid research. This work demonstrates conclusively that solvatochromic CQDs have enormous potential for use in analytical detection and biological imaging.

P-phenylenediamine (PPD), manganese chloride (MnCl2), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), Tris-HCl buffer, H2O2, potassium chloride (KCl), all amino acids, all metal ions, all anions, and other chemical reagents were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (≥ 99.7%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), chloroform, capryl alcohol (CA), and other organic solvents were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). All chemicals and solvents are analytical reagent (AR). BODIPY 493/503 was from Yuanye Bio-Technology Company (Shanghai, China). 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was acquired from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) was gained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical grade or chromatography grade, which were used directly. Ultrapure water (≥ 18.25 MΩ·cm) from Molecular Ultrapure Water System (Shanghai, China) was used throughout the experiment.

The RS-CQDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. 0.4 mmol (0.0432 g) PPD and 0.4 mmol (0.0503 g) MnCl2 were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, and then 15.2 μL concentrated HCl with the concentration of 36.5% was added into the above mixture to form a homogeneous solution. After the solution was treated at 200 °C for 12 h in the autoclave reactor, the crude solution was filtered by the qualitative filter paper. The obtained solution was then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (100 Da) for 2 h, changing ultrapure water every half hour. Finally, the RS-CQDs solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-1011), atomic force microscope (AFM, Veeco Nanoscope Quadrex, Bruker), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer, Paragon 1000), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, AXIS ULTRA DLD), UV–vis spectrophotometry (Varian, Cary 300), fluorescence spectrometry (Varian, Cary Eclipse), zeta potential (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS90), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, PerkinElmer, Avio 500), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TA Instrument, Q50), and fluorescence lifetimes (Edinburgh, FLS920) were employed to characterize RS-CQDs, and the correlative instrument information and specific parameters referred our published papers (Wang et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2021). A Bruker Minispec MQ60 TD NMR broadband spectrometer (60 MHz, Germany) was applied to measure r
1 and r
2 relaxations. Photographs of zebrafish embryos and zebrafish larvae were taken with an Inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, IX73).

Fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of RS-CQDs was calculated by the following formula (Zhang et al., 2021):

where Grad and η are the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance and the refractive index, respectively. The subscripts "ST" and "X" represent the standard and the sample RS-CQDs, respectively. The fluorescein dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH with the FQY of 0.79 (Xiao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021) was served as the standard to calculate the FQYs of RS-CQDs in chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), water, and CA at λex of 490 nm. The absorbance in the 10-mm path-length fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.10 at 490 nm to minimize the self-absorption effect and improve the accuracy. The refractive indices of chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), water, CA, and 0.1 M NaOH are 1.444, 1.342, 1.333, 1.480, 1.362, 1.362, 1.333, 1.428, and 1.473, respectively. The integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance were operated on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer and a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water and Sn4+ by RS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of the lyophilized RS-CQDs powder was dissolved in absolute ethanol to obtain RS-CQDs solution (4.0 mg·mL−1) for further use. The fluorescence intensities of RS-CQDs (0.1 mg·mL−1) in different water-ethanol solutions with the water contents of 0.0%, 9.8%, 19.5%, 29.3%, 39.0%, 48.8%, 58.5%, 68.3%, 78.1%, 87.8%, and 97.6% were measured at the excitation wavelength of 485 nm.

The methods for detecting Sn4+ are as follows. Fourteen kinds of metal ions (Sn4+, Sn2+, La3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Ag+) and six kinds of anions (I-, SO3 2-, H2PO4 -, Cl-, Br-, and F-) at a concentration of 0.1 M were prepared for further use. In order to evaluate the selectivity of RS-CQDs to Sn4+, the prepared ions (50 μM) were added into RS-CQDs (0.1 mg·mL−1 dissolved in the mixtures of water and ethanol (1:1)) to measure the PL intensities. To evaluate the interference of various metal ions and anions to Sn4+, 0.5 mM of the various ions were respectively mixed with RS-CQDs (0.1 mg·mL−1 dissolved in the mixtures of water and ethanol (1:1)) to estimate the PL intensities, and then Sn4+ (50 μM) was added to the above mixture to obtain the PL intensities. In order to get the linear range and detection limit of Sn4+, the fluorescence titration of RS-CQDs with Sn4+ was performed. A certain amount of Sn4+ was added into RS-CQDs solution (0.1 mg·mL−1 dissolved in the mixtures of water and ethanol (1:1)) in the cuvette with a microsyringe to record the PL intensities within 30 s. All data were handled by Origin software. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the signal-to-noise (S/N) with a formula of LOD = 3 N/S, where N refers to noise and S is the sensitivity of the prober.

Based on the linear equation, the content and recovery rate of Sn4+ in the actual samples were calculated by measuring the PL intensities without and with different concentrations of Sn4+. All of the above tests were performed with λex and λem of 485 nm and 505–850 nm, respectively.
Real water samples (tap water, river water, and mineral water) were filtered by a 0.22 µm filtered membrane and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min for further use (Tabaraki et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity and intracellular colocalization imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cell viability was determined by the MTT assay. Briefly, the human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells were seeded in the 96-well cell culture clusters at a density of 1 × 104 cells/hole and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in air for 24 h. Then, the cells were exposed to RS-CQDs with various concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. After the culture medium was discarded and washed three times by Dulbecco’s Phosphate Buffered Saline (DPBS), 10 μL MTT solution was added to each well and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Finally, the optical density (OD) of each well was measured at 490 nm and the cell viability was calculated (Zhang et al., 2021).

Detailed intracellular colocalization imaging was obtained as follows. Firstly, HeLa cells were inoculated in a confocal dish and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in air for 24 h to make the cells completely adhere to the wall. Secondly, the culture medium was replaced by the culture medium containing RS-CQDs solution (70 μg·mL−1) and then the cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in air for 2 h. After the culture medium was discarded, the HeLa cells in the confocal dish were washed three times by DPBS. Thirdly, BODIPY 493/503 (1.0 μM) was added in HeLa cells to incubate for 10 min, and then the cells were washed by DPBS for three times. Fourthly, DAPI (10 μg·mL−1) was used to incubate the cells for 5 min. After the cells were rinsed with DPBS for three times, the confocal dish was placed under a laser scanning confocal microscope (ZEISS LSM 710, Germany) to observe the cell morphology and fluorescence under the lasers of 405 nm and 488 nm.

All animal experiments were approved by the Committee of Scientific Research of Shanxi University and strictly carried out in accordance with the relevant regulations. The approval No. of Affidavit of Approval of Animal Use Protocol is SXULL2021024. Four-week-old female C57BL/6 mice were acquired from Shanxi Medical University Laboratory Animal Center (Shanxi, China). After the mice were fed normally for three days to adapt to their new surroundings, they were randomly assigned to three groups: normal feeding (G1) group with the regular diet for 8 weeks, high-fat feeding (G2) group with high-fat diet for 8 weeks, and NO2-exposure (G3) group with the treatment of NO2 (2.5 ppm, 5 h·day−1) for 4 weeks. Representative excised liver tissues from the three groups were stained with Oil Red O solution and RS-CQDs (100 μg·mL−1 for 0.5 h) for liver tissue imaging by an Inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, IX73, Japan) and a laser scanning confocal microscope (ZEISS LSM 710, Germany), respectively.

All zebrafish experiments were approved by the Committee of Scientific Research of Shanxi University and strictly carried out in accordance with the relevant regulations. The approval No. of Affidavit of Approval of Animal Use Protocol is SXULL2021024. Zebrafish embryos were obtained from the Environmental and Health Research Center of Shanxi University. Zebrafish embryos were placed in a Petri dish (35 mm (diameter) × 14 mm (height)) containing E3 medium and incubated at 28 ℃ for 4 h. Zebrafish embryos were then transferred to different concentrations of RS-CQDs solutions (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg·mL−1), and the hatching rate of 72 hpf (one hour post fertilization) and mortality rate of 96 hpf (one hour post fertilization) were calculated. Thirty zebrafish embryos of each concentration were equally distributed into three Petri dishes.

The zebrafish embryos (4 hpf) in a Petri dish were incubated with different concentrations of RS-CQDs (0, 10, 30, 50, 70, and 100 μg·mL−1) at 28 oC for 1 h. Zebrafish embryos (4 hpf) were cultured with RS-CQDs (70 μg·mL−1) in Petri dishes for 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min. Zebrafish embryos (4 hpf) were incubated sequentially with RS-CQDs (70 μg·mL−1) and BODIPY 493/503 (1.0 μg·mL−1) for 1 h and 10 s, respectively. All zebrafish embryos incubated with RS-CQDs were imaged directly under an Inverted fluorescence microscope, while zebrafish embryos incubated with BODIPY 493/503 were washed three times with DPBS prior to imaging.

Zebrafish larvae (72 hpf) in Petri dishes were treated with different concentrations of RS-CQDs (0, 10, 30, 50, 70, and 100 μg·mL−1) at 28 oC for 1 h. The RS-CQDs (70 μg·mL−1) was applied to treat zebrafish larvae (72 hpf) for 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 min. The zebrafish larvae (72 hpf) in a Petri dish were processed sequentially by RS-CQDs (70 μg·mL−1) and BODIPY 493/503 (1.0 μg·mL−1) for 2 h and 10 min, respectively. Similarly, zebrafish larvae treated with RS-CQDs were not rinsed, but larvae treated with BODIPY 493/503 were rinsed three times with DPBS. They were imaged under an Inverted fluorescence microscope.

Using PPD, MnCl2, and HCl as reaction precursors, RS-CQDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method. In order to prepare the optimal RS-CQDs, the ratio of reaction precursors and reaction time were investigated in depth, and the results are depicted in Fig. S1. The optimum preparation conditions for RS-CQDs with the highest fluorescence quantum yield (FQY, 3.27% in ethanol) are as follows: the molar ratio of MnCl2 and PPD, HCl concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time is 1:1 (Fig. S1a), 182.4 mM (Fig. S1b), 200 oC, and 12 h (Fig. S1c), respectively, which were used to fabricate RS-CQDs in large quantities for further use.

TEM, HRTEM, and AFM were used to evaluate the morphology and size of RS-CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, RS-CQDs are uniformly dispersed on a copper grid covered with an ultrathin carbon film with 0.28 nm lattice spacing. The particle size distribution of RS-CQDs is 2.15–3.79 nm, with an average particle size of 2.85 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 1b). It is evident from Fig. 1c that RS-CQDs are uniformly dispersed on the silicon substrate with an average height of ca. 2.5 nm. In addition, the energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDXA) spectrum (Fig. 1d) and element mapping (Fig. 1e) were used to analyze RS-CQDs, revealing that they are composed of C, N, O, Cl, and Mn. The XRD pattern of RS-CQDs displayed a peak at approximately 21° (Fig. S1d), which corresponds to the graphitic structure with interlayer spacing (002) of 0.45 nm (Guo et al., 2022). The enlarged interlayer distance of RS-CQDs compared to 0.335 nm of graphite may be ascribed to the steric hindrance of functional groups on the sp2-carbon plan edge or the plan distortion of sp3-C in the sp2-carbon domain (Guo et al., 2022). Four obvious weak peaks at 42.7°, 48.7°, 53.5°, and 66.9° are associated with manganese oxides (Bao et al., 2020), indicating that a small amount of manganese was unavoidably oxidized and doped into RS-CQDs during the preparation process, as has been reported in numerous articles of a similar nature (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019a). These results demonstrate that RS-CQDs were successfully prepared and have a spherical-like structure with a nanocrystalline core composed of graphitic sp2-carbon atoms and sp3-carbon defects.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to investigate the chemical structure and surface functional groups of RS-CQDs. The XPS survey scan of RS-CQDs in Fig. 2a shows five typical peaks at 639.0, 529.0, 397.0, 282.0, and 196.0 eV, corresponding to Mn 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and Cl 2p, respectively. Five peaks in the C 1 s XPS spectrum of RS-CQDs (Fig. 2b) at 284.1, 284.5, 284.9, 285.4, and 286.0 eV are associated with CC, C-Mn, C-C, C-N, and C-O, respectively (Tian et al., 2021). N 1 s XPS spectrum of RS-CQDs (Fig. S2a) shows CN and N-H at 399.5 and 400.3 eV, respectively (Li et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020b). Two peaks at 532.5 and 531.5 eV in O 1 s XPS spectrum of RS-CQDs (Fig. S2b) correspond to CO and C-O, respectively (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). In Fig. 2c, the Cl 2p XPS spectrum of RS-CQDs can be dissociated into five peaks at 197.9, 198.4, 198.9, 199.7, and 200.5 eV, attributing to Cl-Mn, Cl-N, Cl-O, Cl-C, and Cl-C-H, respectively (Wang et al., 2019b; Zhu et al., 2020). Mn 2p XPS spectrum of RS-CQDs (Fig. 2d) is deconvoluted into five peaks at 640.9, 641.9, 643.2, 646.9, and 653.8 eV, representing Mn 2p3/2, Mn-OH, Mn-Cl, Mn-C, and Mn-O, respectively (Jia et al., 2018). In addition, FTIR (Fig. 2e) was used to further verify the chemical composition and functional groups of RS-CQDs. Three distinct absorption peaks at 3425, 1605, and 1405 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, CO, and C-O, respectively (Li et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020b; Liu et al., 2022a). Three nitrogen-containing peaks at 820, 1050, and 1517 cm−1 are assigned to νN-H, νC-N, and νC
N, respectively (Liu et al., 2020b; Ravichandiran et al., 2019; Wang and Chen, 2018). Moreover, two important functional groups, νC-Cl (509 cm−1) and νMn-O (574 cm−1) can be found in the FTIR spectrum of RS-CQDs (Woo et al., 2019) to demonstrate the presence of Cl and Mn in RS-CQDs. Finally, the longitudinal (r
1 ) and transverse (r
2 ) relaxations of RS-CQDs were investigated to demonstrate the successful doping of Mn in RS-CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 2f, the r
1 and r
2 relaxations of RS-CQDs were calculated to be 5.38 × 10−3 M−1 s−1 and 74.18 × 10−3 M−1 s−1 according to the Mn content of 22.10 wt% (ICP-OES), respectively, demonstrating that Mn was not only doped into RS-CQDs but also endowed RS-CQDs with MRI function. Furthermore, an ultra-low r
1 /r
2 (0.07) indicates that the great potential of RS-CQDs as a T
2 contrast agent. All results fully illustrate that the successful doping of N, Cl, and Mn can enrich the functional groups of RS-CQDs, endowing RS-CQDs with many outstanding characteristics, such as remarkable fluorescence performance, excellent MRI function, specific sensing ability, and good biocompatibility.

Considering the unique structure and doping composition, we have investigated the optical properties of RS-CQDs in greater depth and have discovered some novel characteristics. To comprehend the optical behavior of RS-CQDs, their spectral properties were investigated in seven common solvents: chloroform, acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water. As shown in Fig. 3a, the photoluminescence (PL) wavelengths of RS-CQDs in chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water at their optimal excitation wavelengths are 562, 562, 568, 587, 604, 614, and 624 nm, respectively. The photographs of RS-CQDs in various solvents taken under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 3b) correspond to their respective PL spectra (Fig. 3a), which range from yellow to red fluorescence. The RS-CQDs exhibit stable yellow fluorescence in most organic solvents (chloroform, ACN, DMF, and DMSO); however, when dispersed in polar protic solvents such as ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water, a red-shift from yellow to red fluorescence was observed (Fig. 3a-b). Both dipole-dipole and hydrogen bonding (HB) interactions can theoretically contribute to observed redshifts in fluorescence (Gao et al., 2018; Sciortino et al., 2016). For a deeper understanding of the fluorescence properties of RS-CQDs in different solvents, we obtained the PL spectra of RS-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths; the results are depicted in Figs. 3c-3i. The PL spectra of RS-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths in various solvents including chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water display an excitation-independent PL feature with emission peaks centered at around 560–562, 560–563, 565–575, 587–588, 600–607, 606–614, and 614–624 nm, respectively, which may be attributed to the π* →π transition of the graphitic structure of the carbon cores (Gong et al., 2019). Using fluorescein (Fig. S3a) as a standard, the FQYs of RS-CQDs in chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water are 4.85% (Fig. S3b), 5.41% (Fig. S3c), 7.51% (Fig. S3d), 7.63% (Fig. S3e), 3.27% (Fig. S3f), 1.89% (Fig. S3g), and 0.53% (Fig. S3h), respectively. The average fluorescence lifetimes of RS-CQDs in chloroform, ACN, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water are 11.35, 8.37, 9.34, 8.89, 6.16, 3.03, and 2.08 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3j and Table S1. In different solvents, the PL spectra, FQY, and fluorescence lifetimes of RS-CQDs are distinct, indicating a significant solvent effect (Wu et al., 2019). In addition, we infer that the observed redshifts of fluorescence and the significant diminution of fluorescence lifetimes are due to HB interactions.

Although the absorption spectra of RS-CQDs only display a slight bathochromic shift (Fig. 3k, 512–533 nm, ∼21 nm shift), RS-CQDs exhibit a robust positive solvatochromic effect with fluorescence redshift from yellow (low polar chloroform, 562 nm) to red (high polar water, 624 nm) region (∼62 nm) and fluorescence lifetime attenuation from 11.35 ns (low polar chloroform) to 2.08 ns (high polar water) as the solvent polarity increases. We infer that solvents with greater polarity may contain more HB donors, forming a stronger HB network between RS-CQDs and these solvent molecules, thus efficiently enhancing the π–π/p–π conjugation and causing the fluorescence redshift of RS-CQDs. Specifically, the hydroxyl groups in ethanol or water molecules can form a denser HB network structure with carbonyl and amino groups on the surface of RS-CQDs (Xu et al., 2021). Consequently, solvent polarity and HB induce the fluorescence emission behavior of RS-CQDs, giving them the solvatochromic effect and potential applications in lipid imaging (vide infra).

To broaden the application range of RS-CQDs, the fluorescence stability of RS-CQDs under salt solution, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), amino acid, pH, UV irradiation, and xenon lamp irradiation were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S4a, an ultra-high concentration of KCl solution (1.0 M) has little effect on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs. The PL intensity of RS-CQDs is almost 100% (relative to blank) under the high concentration of H2O2 (1.0 M, Fig. S4b), indicating that RS-CQDs have remarkable antioxidant performance. As depicted in Fig. S4c, adding various amino acids to RS-CQDs had no discernible effect on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs. In a wide pH range (4.0–12.0), the PL intensity of RS-CQDs hardly varies (Fig. S4d), indicating that RS-CQDs exhibit pH-independent fluorescence behavior within the physiological pH range, which has significant implications for fluorescence imaging in vivo and in vitro. After 180 min of continuous UV (Fig. S4e) or xenon lamp (Fig. S4f) irradiation, the PL intensities of RS-CQDs are nearly unaffected, demonstrating RS-CQDs' excellent anti-photobleaching performance. Moreover, thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) was utilized to assess the thermostability of RS-CQDs. The TGA curve was obtained in the range of 25–800 oC with an increase of 10 oC per minute. As can be seen from Fig. S4g, the weight loss rates of RS-CQDs at 25–144 oC, 144–343 oC, and 343–800 oC were 9.27%, 16.99%, and 48.03%, respectively, which may be related to the release of adsorbed H2O, CO, CO2, NO, NO2, HCl, etc. Manganese and the incompletely carbonized fraction of RS-CQDs were converted with a yield of nearly 25.71% to manganese and carbon materials. After exposure to high temperature (800 °C), RS-CQDs exhibit a slow weight loss, indicating excellent thermal stability. These results confirm the excellent fluorescence and thermal stability of RS-CQDs, laying the groundwork for further investigation into RS-CQDs' the application in numerous fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water and Sn4+ detection based on RS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The RS-CQDs can be used to detect water in organic solvents, which is one of the fascinating findings. The FQY of RS-CQDs in absolute ethanol (3.27%) is 6.2 times that in water (0.53%), so we conclude that the fluorescence of RS-CQDs in ethanol will decrease when water is added to absolute ethanol, as shown in Fig. 4a. With an increase in absolute ethanol's water content from 0.0% to 97.6%, the PL intensities of RS-CQDs are gradually quenched (Fig. 4a and Fig. S5a). The fluorescence color of RS-CQDs under 365 nm UV light irradiation is gradually weakened until it almost disappears (the inset in Fig. 4a). Fig. 4b reveals the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of the changes in PL intensities of RS-CQDs with increasing water content ((F0-F)/F0 against water content), where F0 and F are the PL intensities of RS-CQDs in ethanol without and with water, respectively. A good linear relationship between the water content and PL intensity of RS-CQDs can be found in the range of 2.0–97.6% with a linear equation, correlation coefficient (R2), and limit of detection (LOD) of (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0083c(water) + 0.1206, 0.9959, and 0.14%, respectively, where c is the content of water. The sensitivity of RS-CQDs to detect water in ACN, DMF, and DMSO was investigated in greater depth. With an increase in the water content of ACN (Fig. S5b), DMF (Fig. S5c), and DMSO (Fig. S5d) from 0.0% to 97.6%, the emission wavelength of RS-CQDs shifted significantly to the red. The linear equations, linear ranges, and LODs of water detected in ACN (Fig. S5e), DMF (Fig. S5f), and DMSO (Fig. S5g) are (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0040c(water) + 0.5927 (R2 = 0.9940), 19.5–97.6%, and 0.29%, (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0041c(water) + 0.5582 (R2 = 0.9952), 39.0–97.6%, and 0.28%, and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0085c(water) + 0.3277 (R2 = 0.9920), 19.5–58.5%, and 0.14%, respectively. In contrast, based on RS-CQDs, water detection in absolute ethanol has a wider linear range and higher sensitivity. In addition, Table 1 compares the constructed fluorescence probes by RS-CQDs and other reported CQDs (Kumari et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020a; Wu et al., 2017, 2019) for water detection, indicating that our method is comparable to other methods. Compared to previously reported detection methods, the as-constructed fluorescence probe for water detection has numerous advantages, including a longer emission wavelength, a wider linear range, and a lower detection limit, indicating a greater application potential.

Another significant finding is that RS-CQDs can be used to detect Sn4+. Firstly, the quenching effect of Sn4+ on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs at different pHs (5.5–8.0) of Tris-HCl buffer solution was studied. As shown in Fig. S6a, the quenching effect of RS-CQDs by Sn4+ is the same under different pHs, so the water was chosen for Sn4+ detection. Secondly, we tested the quenching effect of Sn4+ on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs in ethanol containing different amounts of water, and the results are displayed in Fig. S6b. When the water content is less than 50%, the quenching effect of Sn4+ on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs is greatest, whereas it is significantly diminished when the water content is greater than 50%. The greater the amount of water in ethanol, the weaker the fluorescence of RS-CQDs. Combining the fluorescence of RS-CQDs and the fluorescence quenching of RS-CQDs by Sn4+, we chose a 1:1 vol ratio of ethanol and water as the optimal solvent for detecting Sn4+. Thirdly, the optimal concentration of RS-CQDs and the response time of Sn4+ were investigated; the results are shown in Fig. S6c and S6d, respectively. When the concentration of RS-CQDs was 0.1 mg·mL−1, not only the PL intensity of RS-CQDs was strong in mixtures of water and ethanol (1:1), but also Sn4+ has the strongest effect on the PL intensity of RS-CQDs. Therefore, 0.1 mg·mL−1 is the optimal RS-CQDs concentration for Sn4+ detection. After adding Sn4+ to RS-CQDs for 30 s or more (240 s), the PL intensity of RS-CQDs remains unchanged, indicating that the response of RS-CQDs to Sn4+ can be completed immediately (30 s). Therefore, the optimal concentration of RS-CQDs and the fastest response time are 0.1 mg·mL−1 and 30 s, respectively, and these values were utilized in all subsequent experiments.

By introducing a variety of metal ions (La3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Ag+) and anions (I-, SO3 2-, H2PO4 -, Cl-, Br- and F-), the selectivity and anti-interference performance of RS-CQDs for Sn4+ detection were investigated. Except for Sn4+ and Sn2+, other metal ions and anions do not quench the PL intensity of RS-CQDs (Fig. 4c). However, under the same conditions, Sn2+ and Sn4+ at the same concentration can quench 50% and 80% of the PL intensities of RS-CQDs (Fig. S7a), indicating that RS-CQDs are more sensitive to Sn4+ than Sn2+. Furthermore, Sn2+ is reducible and oxidizable to Sn4+ in air and water environments. Consequently, the detection of Sn4+ has greater practical importance. When Sn4+ was added to RS-CQDs containing interfering substances, the PL intensity of RS-CQDs could still be significantly quenched (Fig. 4d), indicating that RS-CQDs for Sn4+ detection would not be affected by the tested metal ions and anions, thereby exhibiting excellent anti-interference performance. The sensitivity of RS-CQDs for detecting Sn4+ was finally confirmed through a titration experiment. As depicted in Fig. 4e and Fig. S7b, Sn4+ can significantly suppress the PL intensity of RS-CQDs at 614 nm. As the Sn4+ concentration increased from 0.0 to 121.0 M, the red fluorescence of RS-CQDs under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) diminished gradually (top right inset of Fig. 4e). (F0-F)/F0 and the Sn4+ concentration (c(Sn 4+ )) displayed a good linear relationship (Fig. 4 f), where F0 and F are the PL intensities of RS-CQDs in the absence and presence of Sn4+, respectively. The linear equation, correlation coefficient (R2), linear range, and LOD of Sn4+ were (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0169c(Sn 4+ ) - 0.1117, 0.9974, 6.24–53.18 μM, and 66.3 nM, respectively. When 0, 20, 40, 60, and 80 µM of Sn4+ were gradually added to RS-CQDs solution (0.1 mg·mL−1), the quantum yields of RS-CQDs were 1.89% (Fig. S3g), 1.30% (Fig. S7c), 1.09% (Fig. S7d), 0.48% (Fig. S7e), and 0.45% (Fig. S7f), respectively, indicating that the quantum yields of RS-CQDs gradually decreased with the increase of Sn4+ concentration, which was consistent with the titration results. These results demonstrate conclusively that RS-CQDs can be used to detect Sn4+ with excellent selectivity, interference resistance, and sensitivity. Table 2 compares the published analysis methods or techniques (Frena et al., 2011; Gholivand et al., 2008; Hubert et al., 2010; Morte et al., 2009; Song et al., 2017; Tabaraki et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019c) with the as-constructed fluorescence probe based on RS-CQDs for Sn4+ detection, demonstrating that our method is comparable to other reported methods in terms of linear range, detection limit, and application range.

To verify the feasibility of the constructed method for detecting Sn4+ in actual samples, detailed detection and recovery experiments of Sn4+ were conducted in tap water, mineral water, and river water. Table 3 demonstrates that no Sn4+ was detected in the tested water samples. Furthermore, when different concentrations of Sn4+ (20, 30, and 40 µM) were added to the water samples, the recovery rates of Sn4+ were 95.4–102.2% with relative standard deviation (RSDs) of 0.15–4.41% (n = 3), indicating that the as-constructed method is feasible for the detection of Sn4+ in the actual samples. These results prove that the fluorescence method based on RS-CQDs has significant application potential for Sn4+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism for water and Sn4+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 4a and 4b, water can effectively quench the fluorescence of RS-CQDs, which can be attributed to the following factors. On the one hand, the solubility of RS-CQDs in water is only 1.0 mg·mL−1, whereas it is 4.0 mg·mL−1 in absolute ethanol. The slightly poorer dispersion and lower FQY of RS-CQDs in water compared to ethanol can be attributed to their aggregation in water. Compared with Figs. 1a and 1c, a significant agglomeration of RS-CQDs occurred in the mixtures of water and ethanol (1:1), as shown in Fig. S8a and S8b. It can be inferred that when water is added to an organic solvent, RS-CQDs begin to aggregate, resulting in an excessive resonance energy transfer and π-π interaction (Kumari et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019c; Wu et al., 2019), that quenches the fluorescence of RS-CQDs. On the other hand, swelling and shrinking are common phenomena in graphene-like nanomaterials (Kumari et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019). Under the strong influence of the dipole moment of the solvent, nitrogen-rich and oxygen-rich functional groups on the surface of RS-CQDs can increase the charge carrier density and induce the charge transfer of electrons toward the edge (Zhou et al., 2022). The DLS results confirm that RS-CQDs expand when it comes into contact with water. It can be seen from Fig. 5a that the hydrodynamic particle size of RS-CQDs in absolute ethanol, water and ethanol mixtures (1:1), and water are 3.06 nm (DPI = 0.241), 6.61 nm (DPI = 0.265), and 9.39 nm (DPI = 0.0762), respectively. Therefore, we infer that water changes the molecule conformation of RS-CQDs, leading to swelling and thus to PL quenching of RS-CQDs. In addition, water molecules partially existing as hydronium ions in the multiple layers of RS-CQDs serve as an electron-withdrawing compound (Alizadeh and Shokri, 2016; Kumari et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019), resulting in PL quenching. To sum up, we infer that water molecules can induce PL quenching in organic solvents by destroying the electronic state of RS-CQDs. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes were used to investigate the quenching process, and the results are displayed in Fig. 5b and Table S2. When the water contents are 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%, the average lifetimes of RS-CQDs are respectively 6.16, 3.05, 3.03, 2.33, and 2.08 ns with the decay rate of 50.49–66.23%, demonstrating that the PL quenching of RS-CQDs in water is a dynamic process. In general, water can destroy the electronic state of RS-CQDs in organic solvent through aggregation and expansion, resulting in the photoluminescence of RS-CQDs being dynamically quenched ( Scheme 2).

Fluorescence quenching can be classified as static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), etc., as is common knowledge (Gao et al., 2020b; Kumari et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2017). UV–vis absorption spectra, zeta potentials, and time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were used to assess the possible quenching mechanism of RS-CQDs by Sn4+. FRET and IFE are ruled out because the emission spectrum of RS-CQDs does not overlap with the absorption spectrum of Sn4+ (Fig. S8c). The zeta potentials of RS-CQDs, Sn4+, and the mixture of RS-CQDs and Sn4+ are − 1.6 mV, + 15.1 mV, and + 4.9 mV (Fig. 5c), respectively, indicating a strong electrostatic interaction between RS-CQDs and Sn4+. When various concentrations of Sn4+ were added to RS-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of RS-CQDs hardly changed, and no new peak was produced (Fig. S8d). Inferred from the results of zeta potential and UV–vis absorption spectrum, Sn4+ can adsorb to the surface of RS-CQDs via electrostatic interaction, which does not produce new substances. The TEM (Fig. S8e) and AFM (Fig. S8f) images of RS-CQDs in the presence of Sn4+ reveal that the strong electrostatic interaction between RS-CQDs and Sn4+ may cause the agglomeration of RS-CQDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of RS-CQDs. Furthermore, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were used to investigate the quenching process, as depicted in Fig. 5d and Table S3. The average fluorescence lifetimes of RS-CQDs in the presence of Sn4+ with the concentrations of 0, 10, 30, 50, 70, and 90 µM are 3.03, 3.06, 3.05, 3.08, 3.11, and 3.11 ns, respectively. In the absence and presence of Sn4+ in RS-CQDs, the change rate of the fluorescence lifetimes is less than 2.6%, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of RS-CQDs by Sn4+ is a static process. RS-CQDs may detect Sn4+ through electrostatic interaction, aggregation effect, and static quenching (Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective staining of lipids using RS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Rich in surface functional groups, RS-CQDs are inferred to be amphiphilic and suitable for use as a lipid-targeting probe. It has been reported that CQDs can be used for lipid imaging (Pual et al., 2022), which demonstrates the feasibility of using RS-CQDs for lipid imaging. Due to its structural similarity to lipids, capryl alcohol (CA) is frequently used to simulate the lipid environment in vitro (Liu et al., 2021). To preliminarily demonstrate the feasibility of RS-CQDs as lipid probes, the luminescence behavior of RS-CQDs in CA and PBS was investigated in depth. As anticipated, RS-CQDs are soluble in CA and emit orange-red fluorescence (the inset in Fig. 6a) with a Stokes shift of 108 nm. It is determined that the FQY of RS-CQDs in CA is 5.12% (Fig. S3a and Fig. S9), which is suitable for bioimaging. Although RS-CQDs exhibit weak emission in PBS, a 32-fold increase in fluorescence is observed when transferred from PBS to CA, providing a favorable signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio (Fig. 6a). For a deeper understanding of the fluorescence properties of RS-CQDs in CA, we obtained the PL spectra of RS-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. The results are shown in Fig. 6b, indicating that the PL behavior of RS-CQDs in CA is excitation-independent. Lipids are abundant in living cells (Gao et al., 2021), the yolk sac of zebrafish embryos (Zhou et al., 2022), zebrafish larvae, and mouse liver tissues induced by high-fat feeding (Gao et al., 2021) and NO2 (Letters and Solutions, 1986; Guo et al., 2023), frequently used for lipid imaging research. Based on the exceptional photophysical properties of RS-CQDs in an oil medium, it is anticipated that RS-CQDs will be suitable for probing lipids in living cell, viable tissue, and zebrafish.

To determine the suitability of RS-CQDs for lipid imaging in living cells, the cytotoxicity of RS-CQDs was first assessed in Hela cells using the standard MTT assay. The survival rate of HeLa cells is higher than 90% after the cells treated with the high concentration RS-CQDs (100 μg·mL−1) for 24 h (Fig. S10), illustrating that RS-CQDs have ultra-low cytotoxicity and good cytocompatibility. Then, cell imaging ability was further performed. After orderly incubating HeLa cells with RS-CQDs (100 μg·mL−1) for 2 h and DAPI (10 μg·mL−1) for 5 min, bright red and blue fluorescence can be observed within HeLa cells under the excitation of 488 nm and 408 nm lasers (Fig. 6c), respectively, demonstrating superior cell permeability of RS-CQDs. Red fluorescence is exclusive to the cell membrane and cytoplasm, so we conclude that RS-CQDs with the ability to target lipids can emit bright red fluorescence only in lipids-rich cell membranes and cytoplasm. To confirm the precise location of RS-CQDs in living cells, we conducted colocalization experiments with the commercial lipid dye BODIPY 493/503. Fig. 6d illustrates that the red fluorescence of RS-CQDs overlaps well with the green fluorescence of BODIPY, with Pearson’s colocalization coefficients (Rr) of 0.88, indicating that RS-CQDs have a high level of specificity for lipids. Based on these findings, animal tissue and live zebrafish imaging experiments were conducted to confirm the high specificity of RS-CQDs to for lipids.

Based on the remarkable lipid imaging capability of RS-CQDs at the cellular level, we wish to investigate the ability of RS-CQDs to track lipids in animal tissue. Long-term exposure to nitrogen dioxide (NO2), an environmental pollutant, can significantly increase lipid levels in the liver (Letters and Solutions, 1986; Guo et al., 2023). The lipid imaging was studied in the liver tissues, which were obtained from the treated animals in three groups, including the normal feeding (G1) group, high-fat feeding (G2) group, and NO2-exposure (G3) group. The liver tissues of the G1, G2, and G3 groups were stained with RS-CQDs and Oil Red O, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 6e. After Oil Red O staining, the liver tissues of the G1 group appeared blue, whereas the liver tissues of the G2 and G3 groups appeared red, indicating that the G2 and G3 groups contained more lipids than the G1 group. The RS-CQDs can emit brighter red fluorescence in lipid analogs (CA), so liver tissues in groups G2 and G3 exhibit brighter red fluorescence than those in group G1 (the third row in Fig. 6e). We analyzed the blue or red fluorescence intensities of liver tissues stained with Oil Red O and RS-CQDs using Image-J software, and the results are displayed in Fig. 6 f. It is notable that the liver tissue fluorescence intensities are in the order G3 (NO2-exposure) > G2 (high-fat feeding) > G1 (normal feeding), indicating that NO2-exposure can induce more lipids in liver tissues. The Oil Red O staining results were identical to those of RS-CQDs staining, proving that RS-CQDs were an excellent reagent for lipid imaging in liver tissues.

The capability of RS-CQDs to perform in vivo lipid imaging was further investigated. Zebrafish, one of the in vivo model organisms, has numerous advantages, including small size, simple operation, low cost, high fecundity, and superior optical transparency (Pant et al., 2021; Ravichandiran et al., 2021b). Therefore, zebrafish was chosen as the animal model for a comprehensive lipid imaging study. Initially, the biological toxicity of RS-CQDs to zebrafish embryos and larvae was examined. The zebrafish embryos were collected to incubate in E3 water for four hours, and then the live embryos were incubated in various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg·mL−1) of RS-CQDs solutions for 72 h. At 72 hpf, the hatching rate of the zebrafish embryos was calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. S11a. Even at a high RS-CQDs concentration of 200 μg·mL−1, the hatching rate of zebrafish embryos is still greater than 75%, indicating that RS-CQDs have virtually no effect on the biocompatible zebrafish embryos. The larval zebrafish mortality rate was calculated to be 96 hpf (Fig. S11b). Notably, all hatched zebrafish larvae survived even at a high concentration of RS-CQDs solution (200 μg·mL−1), indicating that RS-CQDs were not toxic to zebrafish larvae. The only time zebrafish died was during incubation, and the mortality rates of zebrafish in all RS-CQD solutions were below 30%, which is acceptable. These results indicate that RS-CQDs has ultra-low biological toxicity, meeting the criteria for classification as “practically nontoxic” by the Fish and Wildlife Service's Acute Toxicity Rating Scale (El-Harbawi, 2014; Liu et al., 2022b), indicating that RS-CQDs is a potential imaging reagent for zebrafish.

Taking zebrafish embryos and larvae as the research objects, we used RS-CQDs to study lipid imaging in zebrafish embryos and larvae. Initially, the optimal concentration and incubation period of RS-CQDs for imaging zebrafish embryos and larvae were investigated in detail. Then, regarding concentration, zebrafish embryos and larvae were incubated with different concentrations of RS-CQDs solutions (0, 30, 50, 70, and 100 μg·mL−1) for 1 h, and their fluorescence images were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope under the same conditions. As depicted in Fig. S12, with the increase of RS-CQDs concentration, the dyed lipids in zebrafish embryos and larvae can emit an increasing red fluorescence, and the red fluorescence intensity is the highest when the concentration of RS-CQDs reaches 70 μg·mL−1. Therefore, the optimal imaging concentration of zebrafish embryos and larvae is determined to be 70 μg·mL−1. Based on determining the optimal concentration, the optimal incubation time of RS-CQDs with zebrafish embryos and larvae was discussed in greater detail. Zebrafish embryos were incubated in RS-CQDs solution (70 μg·mL−1) for 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min, and their fluorescence images were obtained under an Inverted fluorescence microscope (Fig. S13a). Similarly, zebrafish larvae were incubated in RS-CQDs solution (70 μg·mL−1) for 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min to acquire the fluorescence images by an Inverted fluorescence microscope (Fig. S13b). The red fluorescence emitted by the lipids in zebrafish embryos and larvae gradually increased, over time, reaching its peak at 60 and 120 min, respectively. Consequently, the optimal incubation time for zebrafish embryos and larvae was determined to be 60 min and 120 min, respectively. These optimal conditions were used for all subsequent experiments on zebrafish embryos and larvae.

In vivo visualization of lipid-rich structures in living zebrafish using a simple and non-washing staining technique is a formidable challenge. The RS-CQDs have demonstrated the ability to image zebrafish without the need for washing. The patterns of zebrafish embryos and larvae are visible, and a strong red fluorescence has accumulated in the yolk sac ( Fig. 7a and 7b). As shown in Fig. 7a, zebrafish embryos were incubated with RS-CQDs and BODIPY for 60 min and one minute, respectively, causing the lipid-rich yolk sac to emit bright red and green fluorescence. After 120 min and one minute, respectively, of incubation with RS-CQDs and BODIPY, zebrafish larvae also emit bright red and green fluorescence at the same lipid-rich sites. These results demonstrate that RS-CQDs can target lipids in embryos and larvae of zebrafish and perform lipid-specific imaging.

Photostability is another important criterion for evaluating a fluorescent probe, which is examined by continuous laser irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 7c and Fig. S14, after continuous irradiation for 80 min, the red fluorescence signal of RS-CQDs in zebrafish embryos slightly decreases to 90% of its initial value. In contrast, the green fluorescence intensity of BODIPY markedly attenuates to 60% of its initial value under the same conditions. After 80 min of continuous irradiation, the red fluorescence of RS-CQDs in zebrafish larvae barely changes, whereas the green fluorescence of BODIPY fades dramatically to 50% of its initial value. (Fig. 7d and Fig. S15). During this process, neither embryos nor larvae of zebrafish exhibit serious toxicological responses. These results demonstrate that biocompatible RS-CQDs are more resistant to photobleaching than commercial dye and can be used as a lipids-specific agent. Compared to BODIPY 493/503, a commercially available probe for lipid imaging, RS-CQDs have the advantages of simple synthesis, low cost, a large Stokes shift (108 nm), good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability, which gives them significant research and application value in lipid imaging. Overall, RS-CQDs exhibited allure in water and Sn4+ detection and lipid imaging, demonstrating high application potential in analytical detection and biomedicine.

In conclusion, RS-CQDs were produced through a simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of MnCl2, HCl, and PPD. All of the characterizations demonstrated that RS-CQDs possess excellent luminescence properties, good photostability, and a robust solvatochromic effect, allowing them to be used as fluorescence probes for the detection of water and Sn4+ with high selectivity and sensitivity (0.14% water and 66.3 nM Sn4+), wide linear range (2.0–97.6% water and 6.24–53.18 μM Sn4+), and excellent anti-interference performance. In addition, the satisfactory recovery rate (95.4-102.2 Sn4+) in the actual samples demonstrated the viability of the as-constructed probe. Not only are RS-CQDs biocompatible, but their FQY in oil media is significantly higher than in aqueous media. Therefore, RS-CQDs can be utilized as a superior marker for lipid imaging in living cells, liver tissues, zebrafish embryos, and zebrafish larvae, exhibiting superior results to organic dye (BODIPY 493/503). Furthermore, we believe that the developed lipid marker based on RS-CQDs is more competitive than organic dyes due to its high efficiency, simple one-pot preparation, and low cost. This work conclusively demonstrates the allure of metal-doped CQDs in the fields of analysis, detection, and imaging, which merits further investigation.

Water plays a vital role in human survival and industrial production. Deficiency or excessive accumulation of Sn4+ can cause functional changes in the human body, leading to a series of diseases. Abnormal lipid levels in the body are also indicators of many diseases, including fatty liver, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, etc. Therefore, it is very significant to achieve sensitive detection of water and Sn4+ and lipid imaging.

The RS-CQDs can be successfully applied in selection detection of water and Sn4+ with high sensitivity and specific lipid imaging in living cells, zebrafish embryos, zebrafish larvae, and mouse liver tissues.
